paper_title: Learning to Grade Short Answer Questions using Semantic Similarity Measures and Dependency Graph Alignments
core_contribution: A three-stage pipeline for automatic short answer grading that combines dependency graph alignment features with lexical semantic similarity measures using machine learning. The method first learns node-to-node matching scores using a perceptron, then finds optimal graph alignments using the Hungarian algorithm, and finally combines alignment scores with semantic similarity measures using SVMs.
algorithm_steps: Parse student and instructor answers into dependency graphs using Stanford Dependency Parser
Train a perceptron to compute node-to-node similarity scores using 68 features (36 semantic similarity features across 4 subgraph types, 32 lexico-syntactic features)
Create bipartite graph with nodes from student answer on left, instructor answer on right, weighted by similarity scores
Apply Hungarian algorithm to find optimal alignment and compute alignment score
Apply question demoting to reduce advantage of parroting question words
Combine alignment scores with bag-of-words semantic similarity measures using SVM
Apply Isotonic Regression to transform output scores to original [0,5] grading scale
key_equations: f(xi,xs) = wTφ(xi,xs): Linear scoring function for node-to-node matching using learned weight vector w and feature vector φ

key_assumptions.assumption: Dependency parse structure provides meaningful alignment information for grading
key_assumptions.why_needed: The method relies on syntactic structure to improve upon bag-of-words approaches
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When answers have very different syntactic structures but equivalent semantic meaning

key_assumptions.assumption: Optimal bipartite matching corresponds to meaningful semantic alignment
key_assumptions.why_needed: Hungarian algorithm assumes one-to-one node correspondence represents best alignment
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When multiple student answer nodes should align to single instructor answer node or vice versa

key_assumptions.assumption: Linear combination of features captures grading criteria
key_assumptions.why_needed: Both perceptron and SVM use linear models for scoring
key_assumptions.violation_scenario: When grading requires complex non-linear interactions between semantic and structural features

baseline_limitations_identified: Existing bag-of-words approaches ignore structural and syntactic differences between sentences, while pattern-based approaches require manual crafting
proposed_improvement: Combines structural information from dependency graphs with semantic similarity measures using machine learning to automatically learn optimal feature combinations
condition_where_baseline_wins: When student answers use very different vocabulary but correct structure, pure semantic similarity measures might outperform the structural alignment approach
datasets_used: Short answer dataset with instructor and student answers
suitable_toy_datasets: Simple Q&A pairs with known correct answers
Paraphrase detection datasets adapted for grading
toy_dataset_justification: These datasets contain paired texts with similarity judgments that can be adapted to the grading task, allowing testing of the dependency alignment and semantic similarity components
reproducibility_notes: Requires Stanford Dependency Parser, manual annotation of node alignments for training perceptron (32 answers with 7303 node pairs), and specific feature engineering details

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Provides structural matching information beyond bag-of-words
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Performance would degrade to baseline semantic similarity measures

key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Reduces advantage of parroting question words back in answer
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: System might give higher scores to answers that simply repeat question terms

key_components_for_ablation.component: Subgraph similarity features (N0, N1, N2 vs N3)
key_components_for_ablation.role_in_method: Captures different levels of syntactic context around nodes
key_components_for_ablation.expected_effect_of_removal: Would lose ability to match semantically similar phrases with different surface forms

known_failure_modes.scenario: Student answers with correct meaning but very different syntactic structure
known_failure_modes.reason: Dependency alignment may fail to find good matches between structurally different but semantically equivalent expressions
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Dependency parse structure provides meaningful alignment information for grading

known_failure_modes.scenario: Answers requiring many-to-one or one-to-many node alignments
known_failure_modes.reason: Hungarian algorithm enforces one-to-one matching constraint
known_failure_modes.related_assumption: Optimal bipartite matching corresponds to meaningful semantic alignment
